g.data-narration: Welcome to Bubble Sort! Let's explore this fundamental sorting algorithm together.
Bubble sort repeatedly steps through the list, comparing and swapping adjacent elements if they're in the wrong order.
g.text: A simple algorithm that compares adjacent elements and swaps them if out of order.
The largest element bubbles up to the end after each pass.

g.data-narration: Although inefficient for large datasets, bubble sort teaches fundamental concepts like comparisons, swaps, and algorithm passes.
g.text: Teaches fundamental sorting concepts: comparisons, swaps, and passes
Simple to understand and implement - perfect for beginners

g.data-narration: The algorithm compares each pair of adjacent elements, swapping them if the left one is greater than the right.
g.text: Pass through the entire array, repeating until sorted

g.data-narration: The nested loops drive bubble sort: outer loop for passes, inner loop for comparisons and swaps.
Let us trace through an example. Starting with array: 5, 3, 8, 1, 2. Watch how elements bubble up!
Pass 1: Compare and swap adjacent pairs. The largest element 8 bubbles to the end. After pass 1, last element is sorted.
g.text: Compare all adjacent pairs, swap if needed:

g.data-narration: Pass 2: Now we only need to compare up to index n-i-2, since last element is already sorted. Second largest 5 bubbles up.
g.text: Last element already sorted, compare remaining:

g.data-narration: Bubble sort has O(n2) time complexity in worst and average cases, but O(n) if the array is already sorted.
Add a swapped flag to detect early termination. If no swaps occur, the array is already sorted - stop immediately!
g.text: This makes best case O(n) - one pass with no swaps!

g.data-narration: Best case: already sorted array with optimization - just one pass! Worst case: reverse sorted, maximum swaps.
Use bubble sort for educational purposes, tiny arrays, or nearly sorted data. Otherwise, prefer efficient algorithms like quicksort.
Exam tip: Remember bubble sort makes n-1 passes. After i passes, last i elements are sorted at their final positions.
g.text: Bubble sort makes exactly n-1 passes for n elements
After i passes, the last i elements are in their final sorted positions

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: bubble sort compares adjacent elements, largest bubbles up each pass, needs n-1 passes total.
In summary, bubble sort is simple but inefficient. Master it to understand sorting fundamentals before moving to advanced algorithms.
